name: Kapo
description: Kapo, in Hawaiian mythology, is a complex and multifaceted goddess associated with fertility, sorcery, and the power of transformation. She is often depicted as a shapeshifter, capable of altering her appearance at will, which reflects her connection to the wild and untamed aspects of nature. Kapo is also known as a goddess of hula, inspiring dancers and embodying the sensual and expressive movements of the dance. Her power extends to the realm of healing, where she uses her knowledge of herbs and natural remedies to cure ailments and restore balance. Kapo's origins are shrouded in mystery, but she is believed to be a descendant of Papa (Earth Mother) and Wakea (Sky Father), the primordial parents of all Hawaiian gods and humans. She is considered a protector of women, especially those who are pregnant or giving birth, and is invoked for her assistance during times of hardship and transition. Kapo is also associated with the darker aspects of magic, wielding her powers to protect her followers and punish those who disrespect her or the natural world. Her dual nature, embodying both creation and destruction, healing and sorcery, makes her a powerful and enigmatic figure in Hawaiian mythology.
subtitle: Goddess of Fertility, Sorcery, and Hula

keyMyths.title: Kapo's Shapeshifting
keyMyths.description: Kapo is known for her ability to change her form at will, often appearing as a beautiful woman, a flower, or a cloud. This power allows her to move freely and protect herself from harm.

keyMyths.title: Kapo and the Hula
keyMyths.description: Kapo is considered a patron goddess of hula, inspiring dancers with her grace and sensuality. She is said to have taught the first hula to mortals.
keyMyths.source: Arts and Crafts of Hawaii by Te Rangi Hiroa (Peter H. Buck)

keyMyths.title: Kapo and Pele
keyMyths.description: Some stories depict Kapo as an attendant or sister of Pele, the goddess of volcanoes. Their relationship highlights the interconnectedness of creation and destruction in Hawaiian cosmology.

sources.author: Martha Beckwith
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Hawaiian myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Hawaiian myths and legends.

sources.author: Te Rangi Hiroa (Peter H. Buck)
sources.description: Details about the cultural significance of hula and its connection to Kapo.
